title: Hirschsprung Disease: A Systematic Approach
objectives: Understand the pathophysiology of Hirschsprung Disease
Identify key diagnostic features of Hirschsprung Disease
Apply clinical reasoning to Hirschsprung Disease management
Recognize common pitfalls in Hirschsprung Disease

sections.title: Overview of Hirschsprung Disease
sections.content:
**Hirschsprung Disease**

Neural crest cell migration failure, aganglionic bowel, transition zone, RET gene, suction rectal biopsy

**Key Points:**
• Failed migration of neural crest cells → absent ganglion cells in Meissner and Auerbach plexuses
• Always involves rectum, extends proximally (short-segment most common)
• Functional obstruction: aganglionic segment stays contracted

sections.title: Pathophysiology & Key Features
sections.content:
• Failed migration of neural crest cells → absent ganglion cells in Meissner and Auerbach plexuses
• Always involves rectum, extends proximally (short-segment most common)

sections.title: Diagnosis & Management
sections.content:
• Functional obstruction: aganglionic segment stays contracted
• Diagnosis: suction rectal biopsy (absent ganglion cells, hypertrophied nerve fibers)
• RET proto-oncogene mutations; Associated with Down syndrome (trisomy 21)

sections.title: Clinical Pearls
sections.content:
**High-Yield Points for Hirschsprung Disease:**

• Functional obstruction: aganglionic segment stays contracted
• Diagnosis: suction rectal biopsy (absent ganglion cells, hypertrophied nerve fibers)
• RET proto-oncogene mutations; Associated with Down syndrome (trisomy 21)

mcqs.question: Which of the following is MOST characteristic of Hirschsprung Disease?

options.text: Requires invasive testing in all cases
Failed migration of neural crest cells → absent ganglion cells in Meissner and Auerbach plexuses
options.explanation: Failed migration of neural crest cells → absent ganglion cells in Meissner and Auerbach plexuses

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Failed migration of neural crest cells → absent ganglion cells in Meissner and Auerbach plexuses.
correct.deeperWhy: Understanding this concept is foundational for clinical reasoning in this domain.
correct.clinicalPearl: Remember: Failed migration of neural crest cells → absent ganglion cells in Meissner and Auerbach plexuses

coachDx.misconception: While typically self-resolving without treatment may seem plausible, it does not match the clinical scenario described.
coachDx.guidance: Review the key features that distinguish Failed migration of neural crest cells → absent ganglion cells in Meissner and A from Typically self-resolving without treatment.
coachDx.bridgeTo: The key distinction involves Failed migration of neural crest cells → absent ganglion cells in Meissner and Auerbach plexuses.

mcqs.question: What is the KEY diagnostic or management consideration for Hirschsprung Disease?

options.text: Immediate surgical intervention in all cases
RET proto-oncogene mutations; Associated with Down syndrome (trisomy 21)
options.explanation: RET proto-oncogene mutations; Associated with Down syndrome (trisomy 21)

correct.reinforcement: Correct! RET proto-oncogene mutations; Associated with Down syndrome (trisomy 21).
correct.deeperWhy: Understanding this concept is foundational for clinical reasoning in this domain.
correct.clinicalPearl: Remember: RET proto-oncogene mutations; Associated with Down syndrome (trisomy 21)

coachDx.bridgeTo: The key distinction involves RET proto-oncogene mutations; Associated with Down syndrome (trisomy 21).

imaging_reasoning.order_reasoning: Contrast enema shows transition zone; rectal suction biopsy is gold standard for diagnosis
imaging_reasoning.expected_findings: Narrow aganglionic segment with dilated proximal colon, rectosigmoid index <1
imaging_reasoning.management_impact: Surgical pull-through procedure; may need initial diverting colostomy
